Welcome to the Make-Peace Business System in Action with Paris Lampropolis. We've covered a lot of ground in the past seven modules. First, Clayton revealed his incredibly successful business model and how you can adapt it to your own business. We also learned how to make a big splash, how to use the Make-Peace Money multiplier to hone your knowledge of marketing concepts, and how to pitch the deal so that any client or copywriter would practically sell their mother's soul to partner up with you. We covered not one, not two, but three question and answer sessions, and we're still not done yet. Because frankly, Clayton won't be satisfied until he's proven beyond the shadow of a doubt that his system can not only work for you, but bring in boatloads of money for everyone involved. So to help prove this point, he's called in one of the hottest copywriters in the business. We're talking none other than Paris Lampropolis. Over the years, Paris has written control package after control package. Like Clayton, he's made his clients, and himself, a ton of money. And while he started out as a freelance copywriter, one of the main reasons he's become so successful is that he was able to expand his role and establish himself as an indispensable part of his client's businesses. Now, Clayton has gotten Paris to talk about how he was able to make the transition and how you can, too. He'll share with you how to get a client to see you as more than just a copywriter. Key areas to focus on that can leverage into quick profits for both you and your client, and much, much more. Remember, you'll find the transcript on your bonus CD. Feel free to print it out and follow along. Now, let's join Clayton in Paris. Hi, Paris. Hey, Clayton, how are you? I'm good, how are you? Good, thanks. Good. I appreciate you doing this. Hey, I love doing this. Oh, cool. Okay, well, I appreciate it. Okay, so how long had you been freelancing before you did your partnering deal? I think it's about 10 years. Yeah, it's about 10 years. I'd written a lot of packages for a lot of people. I'd gotten a lot of controls under my belt. I had a very strong batting average as far as how many times, how many packages you write end up becoming a control. It's always hovered in the high 80s. So the benefit of that is clients tend to listen to what you say. And one day I called a client of mine who I'd written a bunch of packages for. I had never done, yeah, everything I'd ever written for them was a control, sometimes against stiff competition. And I said to them, hey, I'm going to be in your town next month, which was true. What if I stopped by and we try a little something new? So I stopped by there and I spent a day with them. And the first thing we did was like we pulled out a bunch of inserts that they were writing in their newsletters, you know, where they advertise different products. And it rides along with the newsletter. And just on the spot was like changing their headlines. It was a very easy segue because it was still copy related, you know. And then from there we did something that was not so much copy and just kind of, it's sort of like expanding the person's comfort zone gradually. So if you have people listening to this call who they say, I'm a copywriter, but the client doesn't view me as a marketing expert or as a management expert, they view me as a copywriter. My answer to that is, well, first of all, this is assuming that you do know your stuff, right? The step one is actually learn the parts of the business. So assuming you know that, and we could talk about that more if you want, the two elements I think are number one, risk reversal, you know. I'm going to be in your town, why don't I stop by and we'll try a few things. There's really no risk for the client to say, yeah. And number two is you sort of gradually expand their comfort zone. Are you a baseball fan, Clayton? I'd say on a scale of one to ten, I'm maybe a three. Oh, good. I picked the perfect analogy. You know, hopefully some people who are listening to this are no baseball, but there's this whole idea about the pitcher nipping at the corners of the strike zone. And as the game goes on, the strike zone expands and the empire is calling strikes where a pitch that may have been called a ball early on is called a strike. Because he's just gradually expanding the strike zone of the empire. The same thing with your client's comfort zone. So I mean, nowadays I'm helping clients with everything from hiring decisions, recruiting people, you know, all kinds of stuff that really, you know, on the face of it I would have no business talking to them about. But it started out with copy. From copy, it's a natural segue into other stuff like have you ever tested this mailing list? Have you ever done this format? And then what's your lifetime value of your customer? You know, what's your, you know, just things like that, right? And then the more success you have with them, the more willing they are to listen to other things. There's this funny concept that when somebody becomes a fan of yours, for lack of a better word, they want everything you got, right? Right. So if you look at Dr. Phil, he did a book on weight loss. Well, look at the guy. This is not a guy who should be giving you weight loss advice, right? Right. But people wanted whatever they could get from Dr. Phil. Right. So like if you look at somebody like Dan Kennedy, he started with, you know, marketing stuff. Then he started selling time management stuff. Then he started selling, you know, all kinds of other business related stuff to his customers because they wanted it. And I think the same principle applies here. Sure. And, you know, what you're doing is building credibility on credibility. You know, a couple of observations. First of all, you know, you went in and you showed that you knew what you were doing. You established credibility with copywriting. And you gave your clients some winners. And so when you suggested first, it's almost like boiling a frog, you know, when you suggested a headline change then, it was only natural that they would want to test that because you'd proven yourself, right? And then as you get more deeply involved with the company, you see other opportunities. Like you said, everything from staffing, hiring, organization, marketing ideas, strategies, media, you know, and other things. It just kind of come naturally. And the two observations, it seems to work best when it's not so much a contrived sell, but just kind of a natural process of expanding your contribution to the client. I think that's dead on, Clayton. It's a natural process. Headlines, it leads to, I think the second thing we did actually was phone scripts. What's your upsell? What's your cross-sell? What are you saying to people who call in? Still copywriting, right? Right. And so then from there, it went on to other stuff. And like you said, it's a natural progression. There's nothing forced about it. You're not like pushing yourself on them. You're just, you know, giving them what they want and need. You're not walking in the door and saying, I'm a marketing consultant or I'm a business growth specialist. Right. Well, you know what the definition of a consultant is. What's that? Somebody who knows a hundred different sex positions but can't get a date on a Saturday night. That's great. Great on the advice, Lausie, on the execution. Right. Well, I mean, you know, there's, I'm even Lausie on execution with this stuff. You know, they have to be executing it for the most part. You know, copy certainly I would execute and all that, but you know, there's a lot of things that a consultant will not execute for you. So if you're, if someone listening to this is a client, they got to understand that. However, you know, the whole thing about consultants is that they are a dime a dozen and a lot of them don't know what they're talking about. Don't walk the talk. I've never done it and all that kind of stuff. But again, it just comes back to if you've proved yourself and you have a track record, you're going to want to, you're going to want to try it out. Right. Well, what advantages do you see for the client in establishing a deeper relationship with a copywriter? Well, the obvious thing is, you know, they make more money if the guy's any good, right? And the other thing is it's easy because you've got somebody already there. You don't have to go out and look for someone and try and sort out who's good, who's not good, who knows what they're talking about, who doesn't, who has your best interests at heart versus who's just in it for themselves, you know. Yeah, and getting on the schedule of somebody like you is not the easiest thing in the world. I know that from personal experience. Good copywriters tend to be busy. Yeah, yeah. When the client wants you, there's a good chance you're not going to be available. Who raised the issue of kind of formalizing this deeper relationship first? Was that an idea you took to the client or did the client... I think so. I don't remember exactly, you know, how it went down, but I would assume I did, you know. You know, when you're a consultant and you're giving somebody ideas, if there's no understanding between you and the client, whether it's formalized in writing or it's a handshake or whatever, you know, then there's no residual income for you as the consultant. Then you're just, you know, trading hours for dollars, which is okay, but, you know, the best way to do it is to do it on performance. And if you really are delivering the results, then they're going to be happy to do that because it's a win for them, it's a win for you. It keeps you motivated to do more for them and so on and so forth. Were there any objections raised when the issue first came up of developing? Now, I'm assuming that in your partnering relationship now that there's some kind of... that the agreement has several aspects to it. Is there an exclusivity clause? Um, not... put it this way, we're living together, but we never got the marriage license. You know? We're living in sin. We are, in fact, exclusive, but, you know, it was never... We never both said it's exclusive. We both kind of left the door open that, you know, we could reevaluate if either one of us was not happy with the way things were going. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. So do you have a formalized contract of some kind with this client or a letter of agreement? Yeah, yeah. And as you said, there are different aspects to it. There's... Sure. Um, you know, I should be asking you the questions because you probably have taken this further than anybody and are probably making the most out of it than anybody. Well, I've written quite extensively about it and my feeling is that it'll be helpful for our easy writers to get another perspective on it and to see how it's worked out for you and also how it evolved. Mm-hmm. You know, the time. And the... I have taken it quite a way and I think we're getting ready to accept one new client this year, a new partnering client. So that's going to be very interesting and we'll get to go through the entire process of falling in love and, you know, negotiating who sleeps on one side of the bed and all of that fun stuff. But I think it's cool to get other perspectives. How hard was it to negotiate your agreement with the client? You know, I gotta tell you, it wasn't hard at all. It was very easy. It was very natural. There was no objections. There were no nothing. I mean, it was just, you know, and I think that comes out of having that relationship to begin with. You know, there's an interesting... Do you know Frank Kern, right? Sure. And he spoke at Rich Sheffrin's big thing last month or whatever. I wasn't there, but I got to see a video of it that Frank Kern emailed to people on his list. And he did this whole talk about getting so in tune with your market and what they want and being just so in tune with them and understanding them that you don't even have to do any selling to them. You just say, hey, check this out. I got this. You want it? And they're like, yeah. Because the this is that you've got so resonates with them and so mirrors what they want that there's no selling necessary. Right. And whenever you can do that, that's a powerful thing. It's the best kind of selling, isn't it? Yeah. No selling at all. Yeah. With with Weiss, it was as easy as I didn't work with them for very long. I think maybe five, six months, you know, and I needed to get Martin out of the office to talk to him about some things. And I told him, you know, I we're working so well together and what we're doing is succeeding so well that I think we should talk about maybe really. Taking this thing to the moon and his eyes just absolutely lit up. And he said, I've got an idea. Why don't we, you know, but we can't talk about it in the office because too many are interruptions on. And he said, why don't we go on a cruise? We'll take our wives. We'll jump on a ship for four or five days. And that'll give us plenty of time in the morning while our wives are still asleep. We both get up at four in the morning. They'll give us plenty of time in the morning while our wives are still asleep. Do some brainstorming. And I said, sure. And by the time we got back from the cruise, we had a contract all worked out and compensation schedule in the whole nine yards. And you're right. I think the watchword here is really natural. You know, it needs to be a natural process as you bring value to your client, you know, right? And you know, you use the word partner. And I think it's an appropriate word to use. There's a guy named Richard Koch, K-O-C-H, that's pronounced Koch. I think it's pronounced Koch. And he did a book called The 80-20 Rule and a Nightingale Conan Audio Course. Or the 80-20 principle, rather. It's, you know, it's the Pareto principle, which we've all heard of. But he really goes into depth on it. But anyway, one of the things he talks about is what are the attributes of a good partner? And one of them was you just have fun together. Like, even if you never did any business, you just enjoy each other's company. So, you know, you and Martin went on the cruise. I have, you know, I've gone to kinds of events, family events, my family and my client partner's family. You know, all kinds of fun stuff, right? Another attribute is that you respect the person or that, you know, you both respect each other. Because without respect, it's just not going to work out. If you think the guy's a doofus, I mean, it's just, you know, it's just not going to happen. You know, if you go into it saying, I'm the marketing genius and this guy's a doofus and he needs me because I'm a genius and he's a doofus, well, that ain't going to work either. Another one was a shared history and that goes back to what we were talking about earlier, you know, a few minutes ago. And I think there may have been one more. I don't remember what it was, but in any event, I highly recommend the book on the audio course, the 80-20 principle. You know, I was talking about that a little bit the other day on a webinar. And it occurred to me that partnerships are a lot like marriages in another way. Yeah, the chemistry is all important. And I don't think enough people really talk in the copywriting space, really talk about the importance of the chemistry and establishing a personal relationship that allows you to enjoy working together. I think it's vitally important. I've had a lot of experiences where, you know, the client was a good company and they do a lot of things right and their products are good and all that, but there was just no chemistry. And so, you know, I wrote the piece. The piece did well, but it just, it wasn't fun, you know? It wasn't like horrible, like, oh my God, I hate these people, but it wasn't, there wasn't a chemistry there. Right. And when you have chemistry, it really does. It's like this added thing that just makes things happen better. You know, I think another one, for me at least with Martin, is that our personalities. And this is interesting. I don't think it always has to be that you're polar opposites, but in Martin in my case, we really are in many ways. Martin is very left brain, very detail oriented, always approaches every idea from the negative side. Yeah. Before, you know, I never fell in love with an idea, tries to pick it apart, identifies all the potential problems, no matter how obtuse, no matter how high the odds are against that problem ever occurring. And forces me then to defend my idea and solve all of these problems for him as relating to that idea, right? And even when it comes to copywriting, very precise word choice is important to him, you know? And I, on the other hand, tend to be more right brain than left brain, and I tend to fall in love with ideas very quickly and easily, and only fall out of love with them after I've tried them and failed miserably. And so we kind of complete each other, and in the same way, in a good marriage, the husband and wife complete each other. Each brings something to the party that the other one doesn't have. Yeah. It's like when two people go into business together, you want one guy who's like the idea guy, and the other guy who's like the organizer guy. And if you have two idea guys, well, nothing ever gets done. You get created, lots of ideas, but you never do anything with them. If you have two organizer guys, it's kind of like, you know, no ideas. A few years ago, I spent a little time with Guthi and Ranker. Bill Guthi and Greg Ranker. And they, you know, as you know, they've been just phenomenally successful. Lord, I could only guess how much money they've made, but I know just one infomercial they did pulled in 100 million in sales. Yeah. And I think in given time, it seems like they've got 40 or 50 of them on television. So anyway, I spent some time with them. And Bill, you know, works from his office on his property in Woodland Hills, and he's real easy going and relaxed and very right brain and very idea oriented. And Greg is high energy, get it done, he's execution, you know? And they compliment each other just wonderfully in that way. And the same thing was true with Martin and me. And, you know, there's, I think, a lot of value in finding, getting a realistic perspective on what your strengths are and what your weaknesses are in order to better identify partners that you'd work well together with, you know? Right. Right. You, by the way, Julie McManus wrote an excellent article in the total package not long ago about, you know, what to look for in a client. And the listeners, you know, be a good idea to go look that up on the website and read that because it's the same, many of the same attributes you'd want in a partner in a relationship. And when you went into your partnership with this particular client, I remember when you were contemplating it. Yeah. And it was, they didn't seem to me to be a conscious decision, oh, well, I'm going to turn this into a partnership. It was almost more like you were presented with this thing. It was almost the inevitable next stage in your relationship. Okay. Yeah, like when you're dating someone and it's like, well, we either have to get married now or break up. Right. I guess, you know, I remember, you, the first two people are called, well, actually the only two people I called were you and Michael Masterson. Because you were the guys who had done it already. And, you know, another thing I've learned is that when you want to do something, talk to people who are, you know, you know, you know, you're going to be like, talk to people who were successful doing it. Learn from the best, you know. And so, yeah, I called you up and I called him up and I said, what's, what's customarily done and what do you ask for and what do you, you know, what's a typical arrangement and all that kind of stuff. Can we talk a little bit about the various types of compensation? Mm-hmm. Without getting too specific because there's certain confidence. I'm not going to talk specifically about what I get or don't get, but just if I were, if someone called me the way I had called you X years ago to ask you, I would tell them that you want some sort of base fee. You want some sort of percentage of sales. And if you can get it, equity would be nice. Mm-hmm. When you do name acquisition promotions with your client, do you still charge in advance in a royalty? Yeah, I do. Like the typical, you know, we all learned from Gary Benzavango, you know, 25,000 upfront against a royalty of a nickel a piece. So that works very well on, for if you're working with a big mailer who mails a gazillion pieces a year, you know, someone like a boardroom or Phillips, healthy directions rather. I'm just going to go to my grave calling them Phillips. Yeah, me too. But, you know, that works very well for that type of situation. Whereas if you're working with a client, you know, we mentioned Rich Sheffrin before, someone like that who isn't mailing in the mail like 10 million pieces but has a high price point product with great margins. You know, you're not going to charge for a piece, you're going to charge some percentage of sales. Mm-hmm. And so that's what we do on the front end. And then on the back end, I'm charging, I'm getting a percentage of a back end sale separate from the acquisition things. And is that, are you doing a lot of work on the back end writing promotions for his customer file? I'm not writing them so much. I do write them occasionally but, you know, a lot of back end stuff, you don't need me or you to write. You know, you can get someone who just graduated from AWAI yesterday who's green. You know, if you're doing a promotion that's about an overstock or a scratch and dent sale or something, you don't need the best copywriters to write that. Mm-hmm. If you're doing a $5,000 a year, what they used to call fact service, they don't call them fact services anymore, I guess. Not their email now, but... You know, just in case people listening don't know what that is, you're in a financial newsletter biz and you have a newsletter that's $200 a year and you get people in the front door with that. On the back end, you're selling them high priced email services that are more like trading, trading stocks or options. So the newsletter would just have like your stock portfolio and buy these, sell these and you get updated once a month when the newsletter comes. The trading service would be like, that morning, you got to do this option or this, you know, stock or something and you know, you're going to make a gazillion dollars doing these trades. And of course, you charge a lot more for that. $2,000, $3,000, $5,000 a year, whatever it is. Well, that kind of back end promotion obviously does take a high degree of skill. Yeah, right. You want to maximize your revenue on those promotions especially because you're going to your customers. It turns out to be the profit center of most companies that do that kind of thing. So the major profit center. Right. The front end is basically a break-even proposition. But I thought that's cool and there's an interesting point there. You're generating, you're building their file which as you say is basically a break-even proposition. And for doing that, and as part of this partnering package, you're receiving kind of an override on back end sales as well even though you don't have to write it. But what I usually do is I end up critiquing it or copy-chiefing it. So again, depending on the level of skill required, I don't need to get involved in copy-chiefing a scratch and dent sale that says, hey, we've got these jars of vitamins and the label has little nicks in it. It doesn't affect the vitamin but it has a little nicks so we can't sell it at full price so it's like buy two, get one free. Anybody can write that and it's going to do just fine without me or without you or without, you know, whereas if it's a back end promotion like we just talked about for $5,000 a year training service, well, now you need good copy, really good copy. So either I would write it or more often another writer will write it and I'll be the copy chief. Are those, that's interesting because I wanted to talk to you about that. You get to a point with these clients where your depth in the organization is such that you can't possibly write everything that needs to get written. Right. Especially as the company grows and I know that you've worked with copycubs and in the past, in fact I've met some of them wonderful people. Are all of the copywriter's people you retain and then pass the fees along to the client or does the client come to you with copywriters sometimes? We've worked it both ways. You know, I think we're moving towards, more towards me just doing it, you know, which means I need administrative staff to handle that kind of stuff and keep track of everything. But then I become sort of like a virtual ad agency, which is I guess what you are, although you're not so virtual, you're a real identity with building everything. Yeah, and again, that has been a very natural process. Just a year ago, Martin came to me, Martin Weiss, my financial client came to me and he said, you know, I love the fact that we've really kicking it up on the back end. And sales are way up. And, you know, it hadn't surprised him because we had done this together before. And he said, but I'm frustrated. He said, I've got all of these other projects that I need to get done. I need Honeymoon series for each of my seven analysts. What's a Honeymoon series? When a new name is brought on the file over the Internet, they're just leads, right? And they are subscribing to a free zine and usually downloading a free report of some kind. And then over time, as they bond with you, they become customers. But it costs money to produce leads now. We're spending $300,000 a month on paper click. And so those names come in and it is about, you know, five or six months before we get that $300,000 back for those 50,000 leads we generated, you know, six months ago. So it becomes important to bring that money forward as much as possible. And a part of that. Bring it forward meaning in the shortest amount of time? Right. So instead of six months, maybe, you know, instead of being able to break an even on your paper click cost in six months, you want to break even in three months, you know, or preferably three days. Right. I was just going to say, how about like a day? Yeah, that would be better. See, the way it works is, let's say you were breaking even on a day because that was your comfort level at the time. Now you get more aggressive about getting more names that would be a loss initially. But that you'll break even later. So now you get more aggressive about ramping up the copy so that breaking gets shorter time. Then you can become more aggressive about going in the hole again to get even more names. Exactly. And you just, it's kind of like a dog chasing its tail except in a good way. Yeah, it's an upward spiral. An upward spiral. Thank you. You're welcome. I'm sure, but I couldn't come up with the right metaphor. It's an upward spiral. Yeah. We started in August with $10,000 and a month in paper click and monitored how well the names converted. And it wasn't for six months that we got our first $10,000 back. But by then, we were, you know, ready to move on to $50,000 and $100,000 and so forth. But the Gauntlet series or Honeyman series is simply a period of time, a week to 10 days, at the beginning of that new lead's lifetime in which he receives no promotions, but he gets, you know, plenty of gifts and freebies to really cement the relationship and hopefully cause him to become dependent on the organization. So what would be examples of the type of freebies you're talking about? Free reports. Mm-hmm. Perhaps a free live event online. Mm-hmm. A free video. Mm-hmm. You know, anything that brings value to his life so that he finds himself counting on you to be there for him. Right. And what you're talking about is the original model was, it would be an e-course, right? Mm-hmm. You'd get a five installment, seven installment, whatever it is, and it's delivered as an email. And each one, they learn another piece of the puzzle and then you start selling them. But then what has happened is it's evolved into being sort of multimedia. That's correct. So there'd be emails that are content. There'd be downloadable PDFs that are content. There may be audio interviews, videos that are content. Exactly. Because people like to get their information different ways. Mm-hmm. Exactly. And the other thing is, I'm wondering if this is true. We know that multi-channel customers are the best customers. In other words, if somebody bought something to you through direct mail and then they also bought something to you off an email or the web, that's usually a higher spending customer, right? That's correct. So I'm wondering if it's the same that multimedia customers are better than single media customers? Well, we're finding that they tend to be. But also, customers are not static. They evolve. And so if they come into an environment where you're constantly changing media, they may not be comfortable sitting still and watching a half hour or one hour video online when they first come to you. But over time, you'll see them migrate. And you'll begin to recognize it. Wow, this guy, until last month, this guy only bought from emails. He never bought as a result of a video. But this month, he bought because of a video for the first time. Mm-hmm. And we also see it in the registration rates for the online events that we do, which tend to be one hour long webinars or videos, video events. And also, we've done a couple of teleconferences online with the client. And the first time we did it, we offered the event free to the file. And all they had to do was register to say they'd be there. Mm-hmm. And we got maybe 7,000 people to register. And that was about a year ago. And now we average about 25,000 people registering. Cool. So the more than that we've offered, the more people have come to enjoy them and set aside time for them and use them. So that's really cool. You were asking about terminology. The honeymoon or gauntlet series is nothing more than the first week or 10 days of a new name on file where he gets a lot of freebies for the purpose of bonding him with a company and usually culminates in a low-dollar sale of some kind. Yeah. Yeah, I agree. You know, it's interesting that you mention that because I think it's an ingredient that's missing from a lot of the way a lot of companies do business, the bonding part of it. It's like, I'll give you great value and I will then sell you more stuff. I'll give you great value on your initial purchase or even the initial easing and then I'll sell you stuff and that'll be great value. And a lot of people do that, but the part they miss is the bonding, the creating the relationship, the feeling that this is someone like the guru is someone you know. If you're doing a guru-driven thing, right? Mm-hmm. Like if it's a health newsletter, there's a Dr. Whitaker or a Dr. Rowan or Dr. Williams or Dr. Wright or whoever. How great if they feel like they really know Dr. Wright. Right. You know? And it's more than knowing him. It's feeling that he has their best interest at heart and that he's their advocate. You know, I'm not going to let you make a mistake. It's a great Bob King line from Phyllis Sinanchel. Okay? My job is to make sure that you don't make a mistake. And so long as you're with me, I'm going to make sure of that. Right. And there's also the us versus them mentality that comes along with the advocacy. Mm-hmm. You know, those guys, you know, we both know those guys are out to get you, whether it's the government or the medical profession or whatever. Right. And so I don't require your paris, Lampropolis, no, this stuff better than anybody, but, um, um, but establish that us versus them mentality is just powerful because now this person says, well, Martin's like me. Martin and I are the same kind of guy. You know? And that, that means that when he tells me something, I can trust him where I can't trust the government or I can't trust the doctor. Right. And I can't trust, and you said something funny about that. Martin is like me. That is profoundly important. So I hope the listeners, um, you know, write that down, burn it in their brain. Um, we had an incident recently where the one of the editors who worked with the guru on the newsletter asked, you know, about what do you think the the, um, political orientation is of the audience. And this is for a health newsletter. And I said, look, don't make the mistake of making assumptions about what their political leanings. My guess is that their political leanings roughly mirror the country at large. So you have people on the left, people on the right, people in the middle, but here's something else I bet. I bet you if you asked him, what's Dr. Rowan's political leanings, they would all answer, oh, he's like me. Right. So all the, the, um, Democrats or whatever, for lack of a better term, will say, oh, Dr. Rowan's like me. He's against those greedy drug company corporations. Right. And if you ask the, um, the Republicans, oh, Dr. Rowan's like me. He's against big government bullies like the FDA coming and taking your, your, uh, supplements away. That is profound. That's absolutely right. If you play your cards right, that's exactly the effect that you'll, you'll achieve. And, uh, it's, it's absolutely crucial. You know, we've kind of gotten into, um, marketing consulting here, but this, this is a, is a seminal, um, uh, concept in creating, um, you know, a cohesive marketing plan for a company. You know, bringing people in, we, we, what do we talk to? We talked about bringing people in at a loss. Um, we've talked about how you can use, um, uh, profits from the first month or, or, or six weeks or three months or six months to, um, offset the cost of creating your, your lead file. Uh, we've talked about bonding with those new people. We've talked about creating a sense of obligation by giving them lots of stuff for free and, uh, and a sense of, um, uh, dependence and a sense of loyalty. And, uh, that loyalty thing is really important. Uh, in another way, I think, um, in the financial industry, for example, not all of your recommendations are going to be winners. Sometimes the market's going to turn against you. And, uh, the fact of the matter is the, the, the growth of your company is directly related to how many people stay with you through those tough times. Oh yes. And, um, um, the same is true in the health industry because, you know, you'll go to people and you say, well, this supplement's been proven six ways from Sunday to, to relieve arthritis, but not every supplement works for everybody. That's right. So it's that loyalty that keeps you. Yeah. You know, I, I'm not going, I'm going to tell you something that you already know, but for the benefit, maybe of some listeners who, who don't, um, I know you have all, all levels of experience in your, in your customer file. Um, really people have been around forever, longer than us and people who are brand new. Um, but one thing that, um, I learned a long time ago from Howard Ruff is they buy the first time because they're expecting some sort of benefit. So in a financial newsletter, you know, make money in a, in a health newsletter, you know, cure some health problem or whatever. So that's the reason they buy initially, but the reason they renew is because they like you. That's so true. Yeah. I think that. And so, you know, the bonding stuff, presenting the information in a, um, easy to understand way that they don't have to like work to read the damn thing. You know. I know one of your pet peeves is headline, long headlines that are all upper lower case. Uh, you know, I have a number of pet peeves about, you know, writing that isn't clear. Um, and you know, how dare we make people work to understand what we're saying? You know, these good people gave us our money, gave us their money rather. Wonder what Freud would say about that one. Yeah, I was about to say. Now that is a copy of my connection. John Carlton say the universe is out of balance until that bastard gives me his money. When I'm writing the copy, I'm thinking that that's I'm picturing that guy sitting on his couch and he better whip out his wallet. I tell my writers, I said, that prospect has your money in his pocket. You're right. Right. And your job is to go get it. But getting back to the point I was making, um, these people gave us their money. Okay. We owe it to them to give them something good. And how dare we make them work at it? Mm hmm. They already worked. They worked the first time when they earned the money. You know, that's right. It's like, how do you feel if you go into some business, you know, local business, a restaurant or whatever. And like you're the one who has to go up and go to the station and get napkins because the waiter disappeared. Or worse, I've had to go into the kitchen to find the waiter. Right. Yeah. Well, you're absolutely right. And the fact of the matter is they won't work. Yeah. You just lose them. That's, you know, that's. Yeah. And so that's like, that's like a big, big loss. Mm hmm. Heck, you already got the damn customer. You know, that was the hard part. Now just, you know, keep the relationship and sell them more stuff that they want. And every customer that drops off, that's like a huge wasted opportunity. So, yeah, definitely. How much should you be willing to spend to keep a customer? I think it varies, you know, by the business. What's the lifetime value and all that stuff. Mm hmm. Do you have a formula you use like a percentage or? No, I think, you know, the quick, easy answer is you should be willing to spend as much to keep a customer as you spend to acquire them in the first place. Uh huh. At a very minimum. That's a good rule of thumb, I would say. Yeah. But you know, if, but I think your answer is actually a better one. If you dig deeper and look at what the value of that customer would be to you if he's saved over a typical lifetime, say seven years, you should be willing to spend a substantial amount of that money. Mm hmm. Because by keeping him, you're not just keeping him. He, you know, he's going to be telling everybody that'll listen about what a great company you are. Yeah. Something people don't understand is, um, that's very important. And if you're going to be a marketing consultant, you need to know this one too, is that it's way better. Well, put it this way. I'll start it the other way around. You can do everything right and never have a complaint and this and that, and they're going to be pleased. Okay. But they're not going to rave about you. Whereas if you screw up and then make it good, now they're going to rave about you to everybody. Yep. Yep. That's absolutely true. So, um, I'm not saying, you know, go screw up on purpose to use it as a way to fix things to get people to rave about you. But what I am saying is, you know, be on the lookout for stuff like that and fix them. And apologize for them, take ownership of them, fix them, and then go above and beyond fixing them. And you will have the most loyal fan, cheerleader you can imagine if you do that. That, and you know, that's brilliant. It's absolutely right. And it's, you know, if a client comes to you and says, you know, how do I increase the response of my promotions to my customer file? I don't know a single copywriter that would say, um, well, first let me look at customer service. Yeah. You know? And it, it, what you said is exactly right. If customer service is scripted to treat complaints like solid gold, then not only have you saved those customers, but it goes viral because they're going to tell their friends. You know? Customer service is the big black hole in American business. Yep. It's like so, you know, so few do it right. And a lot of people think they're doing it right. And people, they're always shocked when they find out what goes on, right? Yep. They say, oh yeah, we got a good customer service and we do this and that and the other, and then you start monitoring it, you know, mystery shopping and stuff like that. And they're always like stunned. Stunned. It's what's going on. When I first went to work with Martin, um, and I, you know, we, we had developed a deeper relationship. I wasn't just freelancing, but it was very early in the relationship. I said, you know, I was just sitting around with nothing to do. And I thought, you know, I wonder, I wonder how his customer service handle stuff. So I called up and I, and I said, hi, um, yeah, I've got this promo here for something called safe money report. And it looks kind of interesting. Um, but, um, I don't have it really, you know, right here. What was the price for one year again for a one year subscription? And the woman goes 99 bucks. And there was this long silence. And I said, oh, okay. Well, what do I get with that? Well, there's a couple of free gifts that you get with it. And she gave me a cursory description and I said, Oh, well, is that your best deal? Is that what I should take? And she said, well, we have a two year offer to it's, you know, you save money on the thing you save a few bucks and you get a couple extra gifts. And I said, well, what are the gifts? I did drag it out of her, right? Right. And so then after about a 15 minute conversation, I said, um, so, um, all right. Tell me about the one year in the two year. I said, you know, it sounds really good. I think I'd like three years. What do I get with three years? We don't sell three years. Was that true? Yeah. No. No. And if it even if it had been true, customer service should have been a heck of a lot sharper than that and said, I tell you something. What you described just now is typical. But let me tell you even worse what happens. And you know what I'm about to say, right? The ones that talk you out of the sale. Yeah, right. The ones who say stuff like, well, you know what, you should just, you should just go for the one year. The two years, no great shakes. You just get a few more reports, you know, or whatever. Yeah. And you know, there's people out there who think capitalism is evil. Taking people, you're taking people's money. Right. People out there who have a scarcity mentality. Right. Yeah. And you don't want those people working in your customer service. And you know what, they are working in your customer service and they are working in your marketing department. And they're the people who are going to be telling you, oh, we don't want to contact our customers more than once a week. You know, we don't want to bother them. Right. You know. And you know, when I hear that, I say, oh, here's somebody who should probably be a plumber. They should not be in marketing. You know, if they'd be a plumber, they'd go broke too because they would be like in me. You know, the guy who comes and does my landscaping, I shouldn't even call it landscaping, the guy who mows my lawn. It's like, I just want this, this, this. I just, you know, just make everything nice. And he's like afraid to ask me because, you know, he doesn't want to take my money. Well, let me decide if I want to give you my money or not. Right. You know, I don't want my lawn looking like crap, which it does. But he doesn't want to come across as a salesman. Yeah, right. Like, we have this hill, right? And it's got juniper ground cover that, you know, to keep weeds. But that doesn't work. There's weeds coming up like every time you look, right? And so I'm like, look, man, just, I, you know, doing this two times a summer, three times a summer and cutting it. I want the stuff to be, well, you know, that's a lot of work involved and it costs money and, all right, so how much does it cost? And they try to talk you out of it. Yeah, because it's a scarcity mentality and a poverty mentality. And I mean, I understand it because I, you know, I used to have it when I first got into it. Got into mail order. I started my own mail order business where I was selling $29 books through the mail. Guess what? If you ain't road down the boardroom, you can't make money selling $29 books. You gotta sell like a million of them to make money. Yeah. And you didn't have a follow on product. I remember that story. Yeah. And so, you know, well, why don't you sell a thousand dollar products? Well, catch that kid too, dad. Who's gonna buy a thousand dollar product, right? I just, I just so had it in my head that who's gonna buy it? Meanwhile, here's the irony, right? I was broke, okay? I was brokeer than broke at the time. I was like months behind and my rent had no furniture. Was eating like my dinner would be like a baked potato, right? And yet, I would spend $1,000 to go to a Ted Nicholas seminar. You know, I'm thinking, well, who had, I was so broke, I couldn't fathom that people had money to spend stuff. Meanwhile, I was even broker than them and I was still spending money. Yeah. Yeah. So, you know, it's a big mistake people make. Yeah, it really is. And that's one thing to be on the lookout for when you're dealing with clients' companies. I mean, and that's one way that you get involved in the employee, in the staffing discussion. You know, when you identify those weaknesses on the staff. Right. And the client says, well, why didn't this go out? Or why did it take two months to roll out this winning promotion? Or, you know, and next thing you know, you're involved in procedures, staffing. You know, I've replaced presidents at clients' companies. I've recruited the VP marketing from, oh, what's that, Big Minnesota Mailhouse? Well, one of the... The one that Dan Rosenthal started? No. No, it's one of the major catalogers. But anyway, I recruited their executive VP marketing and made him president of the company, you know. And you just get involved in these things because they need to get done to remove a roadblock or to eliminate a choke point in the company, some obstacle to growth, you know. Right. And again, the watchword of all of this is just, it happens naturally if you let it. But it won't happen unless you, in your own mind, in the copywriter's mind, you have a different vision of where you want this to go. You know, all the years that I did freelancing where I could have said, well, let me see your inserts, or let me see what you sell to customers on the back end, or let me see your package stuffers, or let me call customer service and see if they're helping or hurting, you know. And I could have done that with 10 or 15 major mailers when I was first getting started and deep in my relationship with them. But it took me probably 10 years, too. Well, you know, Clayton, don't, you know, it's easy to say you should have done it, but maybe you weren't ready. I mean, there's a learning curve that the copywriter has to go through to know this stuff to be able to offer it to the client. Yeah. So, you know, that doesn't happen overnight either. I happen to be a voracious reader and always want to learn stuff, and I think you're the same way. Most of the people who are really good at this game are the same way. And so, I read a lot of marketing books. I read a lot of business books. I read, you know, like, you know, Peter Drucker and all that stuff. And I read, you know, all the classics, the e-myth, Good to Great, you know, all that kind of stuff. So that speaks to a lot of, you know, then there's books on hiring and so on and so forth. Now, of course, part of the trick is separating the good books from the bad books. Right. And, you know, you just, you got to be very careful about learning from people who walk the talk. Like I said earlier in the call, you know, I called you and I called Michael Masterson, because you guys walk the talk. I was talking at Bad Corporate, well, not the other day now, it's been several weeks. Yeah, it was a great interview. And, you know, I mean, this is the guy that mailed hundreds of millions of CD-ROMs from America Online. And he says, you know, his biggest inspiration has been Good to Great, you know. And, you know, so here's a guy with a $150 million marketing budget, who's basing his in everything he does with a company on this book that had this profound influence on his book. Well, it was a great book, or it is a great book. And again, if you look at it, there's a proof element within the book. The guy's not just spouting some theory. There was serious research that went into this. It was like doing, you know, like it was like doing a double blind study or something. Yeah. There's, you know, you always, you know, you want the thing to be measurable, and provable, and all this. But, you know, for people getting started, I think the thing you want to start with is just marketing, because that's a natural extension of copywriting. So learn from the best. Learn from Jay Abraham. Learn from Dan Kennedy. Learn from Joe Polish. Buy all their stuff. Devour it. Internalize it until it becomes second nature to you. I think that's great advice. And, you know, just never stop your education. I go to a lot of seminars and stuff, and I buy a lot of home studies, you know, like the DVDs of the seminar, or the CDs, or whatever, when I can't make it to one. And a lot of people, they're sort of jaded, and they're like, I'm not going to spend money on that stuff. And I'm like, hey, no problem. I'll buy the course for 500 bucks, and then I'll charge you $1,000 an hour to get the value that was in the course. Yeah, that's true. If you use it, if you put it to work, it can be extremely valuable. Yeah, I don't think I've ever not made back what I spent on one of those things, which brings another point. There was a study done, I forget where it was, but maybe Harvard Business Reviewers, one of those things, that said that one of the highest ROI activities any business can do is training. So if you've got in-house copywriters, send them to Clayton's seminar. If you've got secretarial staff, teach them like the shortcut things for Microsoft Word and Excel and all that. If you've got customer service people, send them to a customer service seminar. I totally agree. And boy, I tell you what, it's hard as a business owner sometimes, because I'm constantly getting requests from staff to go to these training things. It gets expensive. Very expensive. And you want to make sure that it's not just a junket to Las Vegas. Right. But I've said no exactly once, and on every other occasion, I send them. And like the richest thing, I sent like four or five people. Well, that's great. So that's a hearty amen to what you just said. You know, the big companies that you and I have done work with, the Healthy Directions and Boardroom, what they usually do is they'll rotate. They'll say, okay, you get to go to Gary, the Benz-Avenga 100, and you get to go to Clayton's thing, and you get to go to, you know, and this way everybody gets to do something. Because I mean, if they sent everybody at 5,000 a pop, you know. Well, Paris, listen, thanks a lot for this today. We've already run over by a good 15 minutes. Is there anything you'd like to close with? Learn your stuff to be good at it, and then share it with your customers and do everything we just talked about. And I just want to close that there are like four leverage points as I see it. The first one is marketing. And for the bulk of my career, that's all I focused on, because that's what I knew. But the other big leverage point is hiring. I just can't overemphasize how much of a difference it makes when they hire the right people to do stuff. Another leverage point would be training. And then there's a fourth leverage point, which is the mental mindset or psychological mindset of the business owner. Most ceilings to a company's growth are not because of tangible things like we couldn't raise more money or we couldn't rent more lists or that kind of stuff. It's almost always mental blocks that people have about growing further, getting outside of their comfort level, being afraid to be too big, totally out of their comfort level. So number one, make sure that your client's comfort level is pretty big. And number two, you know, you could do that to yourself too. Try to sort of always do things that make you a little uncomfortable, because that's the only way you grow. Great advice. I'm going to take it. I think you're doing pretty good in that department. It's always good to be reminded though. And as you were saying that, I couldn't help but think about a couple of things I'm dealing with right now. You can never think too big, you know, getting out of that box, trying new stuff, being willing to fail. I love the old analogy about Babe Ruth being the strikeout king. Right. And it's the same with business. It's the people who never fail that are really not producing anything. So. All right, Paris, thank you so much for this time. This is one of the best interviews we've ever done. Thanks, Clayton. That's quite a compliment. You've had a lot of terrific interviews on here. Lots of great content, a lot of things to think about.
